title: A sharp interaction-degree threshold for simulating QAOA
abstract: We identify a sharp interaction-degree threshold for the classical simulation of QAOA with $2$-local cost functions. At degree $3$, classical sampling from depth-$1$ QAOA with small multiplicative error would collapse the polynomial hierarchy to its third level. At degree $2$, exact classical sampling from depth-$p$ QAOA on $n$ qubits runs in time $n^{O(1)}$ whenever $p = O(\log n)$. The hard degree-$3$ instances have trivially optimizable cost functions, so sampling hardness does not by itself imply a quantum optimization advantage.
summary: This paper establishes a sharp complexity threshold for simulating QAOA based on the interaction degree of the cost function. It proves that while degree-2 QAOA is efficiently simulatable, degree-3 QAOA is classically hard to sample from, providing a fundamental boundary for quantum advantage in optimization algorithms.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether there is a sharp threshold for the interaction degree of cost functions that determines the classical simulability of the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA).
main_result: The authors prove a sharp transition: at interaction degree 2, depth-p QAOA is classically simulatable in polynomial time for p = O(log n), whereas at degree 3, sampling from depth-1 QAOA is hard enough to collapse the polynomial hierarchy.
method: The study uses complexity reductions from PostBQP to PostQAOA, circuit transformation techniques to bound interaction degrees, and the Markov-Shi algorithm for tensor network contraction.
model_or_system: The model consists of depth-p QAOA circuits with 2-local cost functions defined on an interaction graph, utilizing a mixing operator B = sum of X_j.
key_observables: Marginal output probabilities and sampling distributions from the QAOA circuit.
important_parameters: Interaction degree (specifically the transition between degree 2 and 3), circuit depth p, and multiplicative error c.
main_assumptions: The hardness result for degree 3 assumes a multiplicative error model; the simulation efficiency for degree 2 assumes p = O(log n).
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the circuit preprocessing technique, showing how intermediate Hadamard gates are replaced by diagonal couplings to auxiliary qubits to bound the interaction degree.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of interaction-degree thresholds, presents a preprocessing method for circuit transformation, proves the hardness of degree-3 sampling via complexity reduction, proves the tractability of degree-2 simulation using tensor network contraction, and discusses the distinction between sampling hardness and optimization advantage.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
